Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed evaluative predicate: adjective/adverbial kametsa plus existential-stative verb asaiki; used as a compact ethical formula.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To live well in balance with family, community, river, forest, and everyday obligations.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; family advice; community meetings; teaching; cultural explanation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Grounded in Asháninka ideas of good living, reciprocity, territory, and respectful coexistence with living surroundings.
First Example Sentence: Ikantake acharine: kametsa asaiki, pamenaje nija jero inchato.
Third Example Sentence: Jananeki ikemisantaje ñantsi irashi iriri, kametsa asaiki.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal clause with locative metaphor: noun ñantsi plus stative saikatsi plus locative-marked noun kotsiroki.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Speech can cut or harm; words must be handled carefully.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Pinkante ñantsi kametsa, te pinkisakotero atiri.
Usage Contexts: conflict resolution; family advice; politics; sarcasm; public speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes the machete or knife as an everyday forest and household tool whose usefulness depends on careful handling.
First Example Sentence: Acharine ikantake ñantsi saikatsi kotsiroki aririka atiri ikisakotake pashini.
Second Example Sentence: Pikenkeshireero paita pinkantaje, ñantsi saikatsi kotsiroki.
Third Example Sentence: Nampitsiki te ikantabetaro kisaantsi, okantiro irirori ñantsi saikatsi kotsiroki.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Agentive finite clause: animal subject pakitsa plus transitive visual verb yameniro plus object tampia.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A wise person observes signs before acting; planning comes before movement.
Usage Contexts: travel; leadership; hunting; workplace planning; advice to youth
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on observing birds and weather before river travel, hunting, gardening, or walking through forest paths.
First Example Sentence: Aririka nonjate Eneki, nokantaje pakitsa yameniro tampia.
Second Example Sentence: Pamenaje kitaiteri; pakitsa yameniro tampia te ijatabetari.
Third Example Sentence: Yotatsiri shirampari ikenkeshireaka, pakitsa yameniro tampia.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative finite clause: animal subject maniti plus negative particle te and intensified speech predicate ikantasanotiro.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Real strength does not need loud boasting; quiet competence is respected.
Usage Contexts: humor; leadership; rivalry; elder advice; workplace behavior
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the jaguar as a symbol of powerful presence that is recognized without self-praise.
First Example Sentence: Ikantake iriri: maniti te ikantasanotiro irashi, ari pinkantaje paita pantiri.
Second Example Sentence: Te pinkantabetaro osheki; maniti te ikantasanotiro irashi.
Third Example Sentence: Shirampari yantake kametsa jero isaikake, maniti te ikantasanotiro irashi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Balanced biclausal proverb: intransitive movement clause followed by return clause; parallel noun-subject structure.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: What is said travels and eventually comes back; speech has consequences.
Usage Contexts: rumor; accountability; community politics; storytelling; moral instruction
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects river movement as a model for social circulation of news, memory, and obligation.
First Example Sentence: Nija ojata, ñantsi opiyaji, ikantake jeba aririka apatotake atiripe.
Second Example Sentence: Te pinkantatsiri kisaantsi, nija ojata, ñantsi opiyaji.
Third Example Sentence: Okemaji tsinane paita ikantake pashini, nija ojata, ñantsi opiyaji.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Personified finite clause: inanimate natural subject inchato with accompaniment verb otsipata and object tampia.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Endurance requires flexibility; one can stand firm while moving with pressure.
Usage Contexts: family advice; conflict mediation; ecological teaching; hardship; poetry
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Comes from forest observation: trees survive storms by bending without losing their roots.
Second Example Sentence: Okenkeshireaka tsinane aririka ikisakotiro pashini, inchato otsipata tampia.
Third Example Sentence: Jananeki ikemisantaje acharine: inchato otsipata tampia.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative personified clause: animal noun shima plus te and cognitive verb okenkeshireero with object kireeki.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Not every value is money; do not let cash replace food, water, kinship, or territory.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Te okantapitsate kireeki; timatsi pashini kametsa.
Usage Contexts: economic discussion; land defense; family priorities; satire; environmental talk
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Contrasts market money with river food and subsistence knowledge central to daily life.
First Example Sentence: Aririka ikobake osheki kireeki, ikantake acharine shima te okenkeshireero kireeki.
Second Example Sentence: Nija jero shima otsonkatakari pankotsi; shima te okenkeshireero kireeki.
Third Example Sentence: Te pamanero kipatsi irashi kireeki, shima te okenkeshireero kireeki.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Agentive finite clause: animal subject otsiti plus transitive verb yameniro and food noun object kaniri.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Protect what sustains the household; pay attention to basic responsibilities.
Usage Contexts: home; gardening; child guidance; humor; household planning
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Refers to household dogs, manioc foodways, and the daily care of planted or stored food.
First Example Sentence: Iriniro okantake jananeki: otsiti yameniro kaniri, ari pamenaje pankotsi.
Second Example Sentence: Te pijate apaniro pashini, otsiti yameniro kaniri.
Third Example Sentence: Apatotake noshaninka jero ikantake otsiti yameniro kaniri.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Personified transitive clause: inanimate subject kipatsi with auditory/cognitive verb okemisantiro and nominalized object oijataantsi.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Actions leave traces; the land remembers movement, work, and damage.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Paita pantake oka, omonkarataji pashini kitaiteri.
Usage Contexts: land use; environmental ethics; historical memory; community law; elder speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Links territory, paths, footprints, and collective memory of work, defense, and past violence.
First Example Sentence: Kipatsi okemisantiro oijataantsi, te ompeyakotero paita yantake atiri.
Second Example Sentence: Acharine ikantake kipatsi okemisantiro oijataantsi aririka ijataje jananeki.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative metaphorical clause: natural-force subject tampia plus te and motion/causative predicate oshiyitiri with abstract object kenkeshire.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A steady mind cannot be carried away by noise, pressure, or gossip.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Atiri ikenkeshireaka katsini, te pashini iñaanatero.
Usage Contexts: leadership; conflict; grief; spiritual reflection; formal counsel
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on the contrast between visible wind and invisible thought, emphasizing inner composure.
First Example Sentence: Te pinkemisantabetaro kisaantsi; tampia te oshiyitiri kenkeshire.
Second Example Sentence: Jeba ikenkeshireaka katsini, tampia te oshiyitiri kenkeshire.
Third Example Sentence: Aririka okantakotiri osheki atiri, nokantaje tampia te oshiyitiri kenkeshire.
